pair_convention.spot_explanation: AUD/JPY at 113.55 means 1 Australian Dollar buys 113.55 Japanese Yen. A rising AUD/JPY rate means the Australian Dollar is strengthening against the Japanese Yen (each AUD buys more JPY). A falling rate means the Australian Dollar is weakening against the Japanese Yen (each AUD buys fewer JPY). Bullish views target a higher number; bearish views target a lower number.

executive_summary: AUD/JPY trades at 113.55 as of 9 May 2026, sitting at the 96th percentile of its 52-week range after rallying 18.1% over the past year from the April 2025 tariff-shock low of 86.05. The pair is the quintessential G10 risk-on carry trade — RBA at 4.35% vs BoJ at 0.75% creates a +360bp policy differential, the widest in the G10 cross universe. The dominant bilateral narrative is the Iran-war energy asymmetry: Australia benefits as a net energy exporter (LNG, coal) while Japan suffers as an importer dependent on Middle East crude for 95.9% of supply. This double-edged commodity dynamic explains the pair's outperformance beyond what rate differentials alone would justify. The AUD dossier flags crowded speculative longs (+93k contracts, 88th percentile) as a contrarian headwind, while the JPY dossier highlights the BoJ's unprecedented 6-3 hawkish hold on 28 April — three dissenters at 1.00% — as a catalyst for differential compression. Our headline view is tactically Neutral-to-Mildly Bullish at conviction 3/5: carry supports the pair near term but the proximity to multi-decade highs, BoJ hike risk (June/July), and potential Hormuz reopening create a wider distribution than carry alone would suggest. Strategically, we turn Neutral at conviction 3/5 as rate-differential compression and oil normalisation erode the structural bullish pillars.

market_context.valuation_assessment: The AUD dossier notes AUD/JPY PPP fair value at approximately 98, implying the pair trades ~15.9% above fundamental equilibrium. This is the richest valuation among AUD crosses at +12.2% deviation. JPY's BIS REER sits in the bottom 8th percentile of post-1990 history — deeply cheap — while AUD's REER is at the 78th percentile. The combined PPP signal is structurally bearish for AUD/JPY but, as with all PPP-based measures, operates on multi-year horizons rather than tactically.

positioning.net_pair_implication: The combined positioning picture implies the carry-trade long is crowded from the AUD side but the typical JPY-short overhang has been worked off, reducing squeeze risk for a sharp AUD/JPY decline while also limiting further upside momentum from short-covering. Any selloff is driven primarily by AUD long liquidation, not JPY short-covering.
positioning.crowding_assessment: Pair trade crowded on long side (AUD leg); JPY short already covered — asymmetric downside risk

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA 112.34 / 100d SMA 111.30 / 200d SMA 107.04 — bullishly stacked configuration (spot > 50d > 100d > 200d), supporting trend-following CTA longs
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Consolidation near cycle high after 18-month uptrend; locally overbought after retesting horizontal resistance at 113.76; daily chart shows upper Bollinger band near 114.75 as cap

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: Realised vol has been elevated relative to IV — intervention-driven moves in USD/JPY spill through to AUD/JPY, creating a positive variance risk premium for spot traders
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Not directly observable from public OTC sources; inferred as mildly skewed toward JPY calls (AUD/JPY puts) given crowded AUD longs and proximity to cycle highs
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Mild backwardation likely given the proximity of the 16-17 June BoJ MPM and FOMC — events that could generate 200-300 pip moves in either direction; options appear relatively cheap for hedging at current IV levels
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Low vol / below median (estimated IV ~10% vs 1Y median higher); cheap hedges available

market_context.recent_regime: The pair has been in a powerful structural uptrend since April 2025, driven by the triple tailwind of RBA hawkishness, Iran-war energy asymmetry, and risk appetite recovery. The most recent phase (March-May 2026) has seen consolidation between 109-115, with the April 28 high of 114.71 representing the cycle top. The 30 April selloff from 114.45 to 112.80 coincided with the BoJ's hawkish 6-3 vote and suspected MoF intervention in USD/JPY. The pair has since stabilised around 113, reflecting the market digesting the tension between still-wide carry and growing BoJ normalisation risk.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 28 pips (0.25% of spot) against before carry is erased over 28 days — a thin cushion relative to the pair's daily range of 80-150 pips

forward_points.1m: -30 to -35 pips (AUD at discount, reflecting rate differential)

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The RBA-BoJ policy rate differential of +360bp is the widest bilateral carry in the G10 cross universe, generating a carry-to-vol ratio of approximately 0.36 — making AUD/JPY the second-most-attractive carry trade in G10 after AUD/CHF (0.54). The annualised carry of +360bp translates to approximately +1.0bp per day or roughly 1.13 yen per day on a spot position at 113.55, equating to a 28-day carry of approximately 31.6 yen (approximately 28 pips). The forward curve prices gradual AUD depreciation vs JPY, consistent with eventual rate-differential compression. While the carry is structurally supportive, the 28-pip daily breakeven provides minimal protection against the pair's normal 80-150 pip daily range, meaning carry alone provides negligible cushion against adverse moves. The forward curve at 3M (-95 pips) and 6M (-185 pips) prices less AUD/JPY depreciation than our expected path, creating positive expectation for short positions despite the negative carry cost.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The RBA-BoJ policy rate differential of +360bp is the dominant driver of AUD/JPY and currently sits at its widest point in the cycle. The RBA delivered its third consecutive 25bp hike on 5 May to 4.35% with an 8-1 vote, while the BoJ held at 0.75% on 28 April with a 6-3 hawkish vote. Over the next 2-4 weeks, neither central bank meets (next RBA 16 June, next BoJ 16-17 June), meaning the carry advantage persists uninterrupted. The AU-JP 10Y sovereign spread of +249bp (AU 10Y 4.99% vs JGB 10Y 2.50%) is the widest in years and supports carry-seeking flows into AUD/JPY. The RBA's 'well placed to respond' language signals a likely June pause, which is modestly pair-negative at the margin, but the BoJ's June meeting is a more material event: three dissenters at 1.00% make a June hike live at ~35-40% probability. A BoJ June hike would compress the differential by 25bp and shock AUD/JPY 150-250 pips lower — this binary risk is the primary tactical headwind to an otherwise bullish carry picture.
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA Cash Rate: 4.35% vs BoJ: 0.75% = +360bp differential
BoJ June hike probability: ~35-40% (market may be underpricing)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price the carry advantage as broadly persistent through June, with only partial BoJ hike pricing (~10bp for June). The mispricing is in the BoJ tail: a June hike would generate 150-250 pip downside not currently embedded in forward curves.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the RBA-BoJ differential is set to compress from both sides. The BoJ is projected to reach 1.00-1.25% by year-end (MUFG, Sony Financial views), while the RBA approaches terminal at 4.35-4.85% with the first cut likely priced for Q1-Q2 2027. The projected end-2026 policy gap of approximately +310bp (down from +360bp) implies a structural headwind for AUD/JPY of approximately 50bp of carry erosion. The AU-JP 10Y spread could narrow from +249bp toward +200bp if JGB 10Y rises toward 2.7-3.0% while AU 10Y stabilises. Each 25bp of BoJ hiking historically translates to approximately 300-400 pips of AUD/JPY downside when accompanied by a stalled or pausing RBA. The strategic carry framework remains supportive (the differential stays wide in absolute terms) but the direction of travel — narrowing — argues for neutral rather than bullish.
key_bilateral_metrics: BoJ projected rate path: 1.00% by Q3, 1.25% by Q4
RBA projected rate path: 4.35% terminal with cuts from Q1 2027
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price approximately 60bp of differential compression by year-end, broadly consistent with our view. The edge is in the timing: if the BoJ hikes in June rather than July/September, the compression front-loads and AUD/JPY falls faster than priced.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bullish reflects the uninterrupted 360bp carry advantage over 4 weeks with no meetings. Strategic neutral reflects the compressing trajectory as BoJ normalises and RBA approaches terminal. The inflection is the June 16-17 BoJ meeting.

carry_analysis.strategic_compression: Projected +310bp by end-2026 from +360bp today; ~50bp erosion over 6M

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Australia's inflation dynamics unambiguously support continued RBA hawkishness: March monthly CPI at 4.6% YoY (vs Japan's headline 1.5%) creates a +310bp headline inflation differential. The trimmed mean at 3.3% vs Japan's core-core at 2.4% further supports the RBA's relative hawkish stance. Growth differentials are modest (AU GDP 2.1% YoY vs JP 1.3%), but Australia's growth composition (mining/services-led) is more commodity-supportive than Japan's (manufacturing/export-led). The Q1 WPI release on 14 May is the critical near-term data point: a print above 3.6% would reinforce RBA hawkishness and be pair-positive. Japan's Q1 GDP (19 May) could surprise weaker given the BoJ's growth downgrade to 0.5%, which would also be mildly pair-positive by dampening BoJ hike expectations. The Shunto 2026 wage settlement at 5.09% is the counterpoint — it anchors Japan's wage-inflation cycle and supports BoJ normalisation, which is pair-negative.
key_bilateral_metrics: Core/trimmed mean: AU 3.3% vs JP core-core 2.4% = +90bp
Unemployment: AU 4.3% vs JP 2.7% (Japan tighter but less rate-sensitive)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price AU inflation as sticky and JP inflation as drifting. A sticky Q2 AU trimmed mean (3.5%) combined with soft JP growth would widen the growth differential and extend the carry thesis.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the inflation/growth picture becomes more balanced. If the Iran ceasefire holds and Brent reverts to $80-90, Australia's energy-driven inflation impulse fades while Japan's cost-push inflation also eases. The key difference: Australia's inflation is supply-driven (fuel) while Japan's wage-inflation cycle (5.09% Shunto) is demand-driven and more durable. This suggests BoJ normalisation momentum persists even as RBA pauses, narrowing the inflation-dynamics advantage for AUD/JPY. Growth risks are more symmetric: Australia faces mortgage refinancing headwinds (average rate ~6.8% post-hike) while Japan faces oil-shock drag on consumer spending. NAB business confidence at -29 and Westpac consumer sentiment crash to 80.1 are leading indicators for Australian growth deceleration.
key_bilateral_metrics: RBA SMP Q2 2027 CPI forecast: 2.4% (disinflation assumed)
BoJ FY2026 core CPI: 2.8% (highest revision ever)
JP Shunto structural floor: 5%+ wage growth anchored through FY2027
Energy normalisation probability: 60% (removes AU inflation impulse)
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets expect both inflation trajectories to converge toward 2% by 2027, which is broadly correct but underweights Japan's wage-cycle persistence. This argues for faster BoJ normalisation than priced, which is pair-bearish.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bullish reflects AU inflation dominance over 4 weeks; strategic neutral reflects the convergence of inflation dynamics as energy normalises and Japan's wage cycle sustains BoJ momentum.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Pure risk proxy pair; boost risk factor +0.02 tactical and +0.02 strategic to reflect AUD/JPY's archetypal G10 risk barometer status

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/JPY is the archetypal G10 risk barometer — historically exhibiting among the highest betas to global equity risk-on/risk-off cycles. The current risk backdrop is moderately supportive: VIX at 17.5, S&P 500 near record highs at 7,260, and Iran ceasefire hopes firming. However, JPY's safe-haven character has been partially compromised during this episode (energy-import vulnerability dominated the safe-haven reflex), meaning AUD/JPY's risk beta is somewhat dampened. The bilateral oil dynamic is the critical risk channel: Australia earns more from energy exports while Japan pays more to import energy, so rising oil prices push AUD/JPY higher, but if the conflict de-escalates and oil drops below $90, the whole dynamic reverses. The Nikkei 225 at 59,513 (+18.6% YTD) provides some positive risk backdrop, but the Nikkei VI at ~29.5 is elevated vs SPX/VIX, reflecting Japan-specific stress. Iron ore at $105-108 is supportive but China port stocks near record levels (~177.5mt) create overhang risk.
key_bilateral_metrics: AUD/JPY correlation with S&P 500 (60d): ~0.30 (decoupled from normal 0.40-0.50)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price benign risk continuation. The mispricing is in the binary ceasefire outcome: a firm Hormuz reopening would remove the oil/commodity premium and trigger a risk-reassessment for AUD/JPY specifically.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, AUD/JPY's high risk beta creates vulnerability. The structural global growth backdrop is decelerating, and a 2H risk-off episode — whether from tariff escalation, US recession concerns, or renewed geopolitical stress — would hit AUD/JPY asymmetrically hard. The August 2024 carry unwind is the reference event: AUD/JPY fell approximately 15 figures in weeks when JPY shorts were covered violently. While current positioning is less extreme (JPY shorts largely cleared), the AUD-side crowding (+93k) means any risk-off catalyst triggers AUD selling. Japan's +$3.3T NIIP provides a structural repatriation reflex during global risk-off — capital flows home to JPY in stress. The oil normalisation scenario (60% probability over 6M) removes the commodity premium that has been artificially boosting AUD/JPY's risk profile above equilibrium.
key_bilateral_metrics: AUD/JPY downside in risk-off (historical): 10-20% drawdown typical
AUD CFTC longs: +93k (crowded for unwind in risk-off)
August 2024 carry unwind reference: -15 figures in weeks
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price benign risk continuation. Strategic option-implied vol likely underprices the tail-risk distribution for AUD/JPY given the pair's pure risk-proxy character.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical neutral reflects the current goldilocks risk regime supporting carry; strategic bearish reflects the asymmetric downside distribution inherent in holding a risk-proxy pair at cycle highs with crowded positioning.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The combined positioning picture is the clearest single bearish signal for AUD/JPY. AUD CFTC combined spec/AM longs at +93k contracts (88th percentile) represent the most stretched bullish AUD positioning in over a year, while JPY leveraged funds at +56.4k net long represent an atypical configuration that removes the traditional short-squeeze fuel. The net pair positioning implication: the AUD-long leg is crowded and vulnerable to unwind, while the JPY-short leg has already been covered — meaning any AUD/JPY selloff is driven primarily by AUD long liquidation, not JPY short-covering. The asymmetric signal — real-money AUD allocators chasing performance (+8.6k WoW) while leveraged funds trim (-470 WoW) — is a classic late-cycle pattern. Historical analogues from AUD (May 2017 at +84k → 11% AUD decline; January 2018 at +75k → 14% decline) confirm the vulnerability.
key_bilateral_metrics: JPY leveraged funds: +56.4k net long (atypical, no squeeze fuel)
Net pair positioning: both legs long respective currencies = AUD/JPY signal muted for upside, amplified for downside
Estimated unwind magnitude: 150-300 pip AUD/JPY decline on catalyst
tactical.what_is_priced: The crowded AUD long is not reflected in options pricing — AUD/JPY puts appear relatively cheap given the positioning skew. The mispricing is significant for downside hedges.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategic positioning analysis considers the structural narrative evolution. If Hormuz normalises (60% probability), the RBA approaches terminal, and BoJ delivers 1-2 hikes, the fundamental narrative supporting long AUD/JPY weakens progressively. AUD positioning should normalise from +93k toward the 5Y mean of +20-30k — a process that mechanically removes AUD/JPY bid. Japanese institutional flows (GPIF, lifers) could provide countervailing support: at sub-USD/JPY 150 levels (implied if BoJ hikes aggressively), Japanese lifers historically increase foreign-bond purchases including AU bonds, creating a structural AUD/JPY flow. However, this channel only activates at lower AUD/JPY levels, not at current 113+ area.
key_bilateral_metrics: Implied mechanical unwind: ~60-70k contracts = 5-8% AUD/JPY downside
Japanese lifer AU bond demand activation: USD/JPY <150 (not yet)
Asset manager lag: 3-6 months before turning
Combined positioning asymmetry: 3-5% downside vs 1-2% upside
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price gradual positioning normalisation. The strategic mispricing is in the pace — a clustering of bearish catalysts (BoJ hike + Hormuz + soft AU data) could compress the normalisation into weeks rather than months.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bearish, with tactical higher conviction (65 vs 58) reflecting the immediacy of the positioning overhang and near-term catalysts (WPI, Labour Force, Federal Budget). The contrarian case for long AUD/JPY at these levels is weak on positioning grounds.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: AUD-long leg crowded (+93k, 88th percentile); JPY-short already covered (+56.4k net long JPY) — net pair signal amplified for downside, muted for upside
tactical.unwind_trigger: A firm Hormuz reopening, a BoJ June hike surprise, or a soft AU WPI print — any would catalyse AUD long liquidation generating 150-300 pip AUD/JPY decline
tactical.historical_analogue: May 2017 AUD longs at +84k preceded 11% AUD decline; January 2018 at +75k preceded 14% decline; both provide strong analogues for current +93k positioning extreme
tactical.contrarian_case: The contrarian case against the crowded long is compelling: at the 88th percentile of historical positioning with the pair at the 96th percentile of its 52-week range, the asymmetric risk is heavily skewed to the downside. The real-money allocators chasing performance while leveraged funds trim is a classic late-cycle distribution pattern. Historical precedent shows that AUD positioning extremes above +80k almost invariably precede significant pair-negative moves. The lack of JPY-short squeeze potential means any correction is driven by orderly (but persistent) AUD long liquidation rather than explosive short-covering, suggesting a grinding decline rather than a sharp reversal — but the direction of travel for positioning normalisation is unambiguous.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: AUD positioning needs to normalise from +93k toward 5Y mean of +20-30k; mechanical removal of ~60-70k contracts implies 5-8% AUD/JPY downside over 3-6 months
strategic.unwind_trigger: Clustering of bearish catalysts (BoJ hike + Hormuz normalisation + soft AU data) could compress multi-month normalisation into weeks
strategic.historical_analogue: August 2024 carry unwind saw AUD/JPY fall approximately 15 figures in weeks; while current positioning is less extreme on the JPY side, the AUD crowding is comparable
strategic.contrarian_case: The strategic contrarian case for short AUD/JPY is supported by the convergence of three independent positioning signals: AUD longs at extreme, JPY shorts already covered (reducing squeeze risk), and Japanese institutional lifers not yet active buyers of AU bonds at current levels. The mechanical normalisation of AUD positioning toward the 5Y mean alone implies 5-8% downside — before accounting for the fundamental catalyst of BoJ normalisation and energy premium erosion. The structural bull case narrative (carry + ToT) is well-known and well-owned, which itself is a contrarian warning signal. Historically, when a carry trade becomes the consensus expression, the distribution of outcomes skews sharply negative as the exit becomes crowded.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Commodity-sensitive bilateral ToT; boost tactical weight by +0.02 to reflect the active Iran-war energy asymmetry as a real-time pair driver

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral terms-of-trade story is the most powerful pair-specific driver currently in play. Australia is a net energy exporter benefiting from Brent at $102-113, while Japan imports 95.9% of its crude from the Middle East — the highest dependency since 1965. Australia currently supplies approximately half of Japan's LNG needs, representing one of the most significant bilateral energy dependencies in the Indo-Pacific. Australian exports to Japan were $25.25 billion during 2025, while Japan imported $43.52 billion from Australia in 2025. The bilateral trade surplus in Australia's favour creates structural AUD demand from Japan. The net commodity exposure using framework direction rules: Oil ↑ → +AUD (mild via LNG) and −−JPY (severe energy import cost) = net strongly bullish AUD/JPY. Iron ore ↑ → ++AUD and neutral JPY = bullish AUD/JPY. LNG ↑ → +AUD and −JPY = bullish AUD/JPY. On all commodity vectors, the pair is unambiguously bullish while the Iran-war premium persists.
key_bilateral_metrics: AU exports to JP: ~$25bn/yr (structural AUD demand)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price the oil premium as partially persistent. A Hormuz reopening (Brent <$90) would remove approximately 500-800 pips of pair premium based on energy-ToT regression estimates.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the Iran ceasefire trajectory is the swing variable. Base case (40%): Hormuz reopens by Q3, Brent reverts to $80-90 — this erodes the energy asymmetry that has been the pair's primary non-carry tailwind. Japan's structural primary income surplus (+$3.3T NIIP generating ~¥45-50T in annual income) provides a long-term buffer against trade-balance deterioration. Australia's Q4 2025 current account deficit of AUD 21.1bn (-2.9% of GDP) is a slow-moving negative. A significant Strait of Hormuz disruption simultaneously threatens Japanese LNG and oil imports while constraining Australian energy export logistics, creating compounding pressure on both economies. The May 2026 bilateral summit saw Albanese and Takaichi sign four agreements strengthening cooperation in energy, defence, and critical minerals, reinforcing the structural bilateral trade architecture. However, these long-term flows do not generate short-term pair direction.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japan current account: +4% of GDP (structural surplus)
Bilateral investment: A$1.67bn critical minerals framework (long-term)
strategic.what_is_priced: The forward curve prices moderate AUD/JPY decline consistent with energy normalisation. The mispricing is in the correlation: energy normalisation + BoJ hike + AUD position unwind are correlated, meaning the pair-negative scenario is fatter-tailed than a simple probability-weighted average suggests.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bullish reflects the active energy-ToT premium; strategic neutral reflects the probability-weighted expectation of energy normalisation removing the bilateral commodity edge.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: Australia: net energy exporter (LNG, coal, iron ore) — benefits from rising oil and commodity prices; supplies ~50% of Japan's LNG
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: Japan: net energy importer — 95.9% of crude from Middle East; highly vulnerable to oil price spikes; modest iron ore consumer (secondary channel)
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil/Brent ↑: AUD mild positive (LNG export revenues rise) and JPY strongly negative (import bill surges) → net effect: STRONGLY BULLISH AUD/JPY. Step 2 — Iron Ore ↑: AUD strongly positive (major export revenue) and JPY approximately neutral (Japan is a secondary consumer) → net effect: BULLISH AUD/JPY. Step 3 — LNG ↑: AUD positive (export revenues) and JPY negative (import cost) → net effect: BULLISH AUD/JPY. Step 4 — Oil ↓ (Hormuz reopening): AUD mild negative and JPY strongly positive → net effect: BEARISH AUD/JPY, approximately 500-800 pips reversal estimated. On all active commodity vectors given the Iran-war context, AUD/JPY is unambiguously long commodity exposure.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Net strongly pair-positive: AUD gains modestly from LNG/energy export revenues while JPY suffers severely from increased import costs; estimated AUD/JPY impact +200-350 pips
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Net strongly pair-negative: energy premium unwinds, AUD ToT advantage erodes, JPY energy import costs fall sharply; estimated AUD/JPY impact -300-500 pips
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Brent crude oil via the Japan Middle East dependency channel — Japan's 95.9% ME crude dependency makes oil the single most powerful pair-specific commodity driver, creating an asymmetric bilateral transmission not seen in any other G10 cross

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both central banks have enhanced their credibility through recent actions. Bullock's RBA has delivered three consecutive hikes with transparent communication, while Ueda's BoJ has managed the most aggressive normalisation in three decades. The net guidance signal for AUD/JPY is balanced: Bullock's 'well placed to respond' language signals a pause (mildly pair-negative), while Ueda's 'real interest rates significantly low' signals further hikes (more pair-negative). The BoJ's 6-3 vote with three named dissenters (Nakagawa, Takata, Tamura) at 1.00% is the more impactful guidance signal — it creates a clear pathway to a June or July hike that the market has not fully priced. The RBA-BoJ guidance divergence has narrowed: in February, the RBA was clearly more hawkish; now both banks are hawkish but the BoJ is accelerating toward the pair while the RBA is decelerating.
key_bilateral_metrics: Guidance convergence: both hawkish but BoJ accelerating
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price RBA pause and BoJ hold as base case for June. The mispricing is in the BoJ tail — a June hike would shock AUD/JPY 150-250 pips lower.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the credibility differential is shifting in JPY's favour. The BoJ's April Outlook Report raised FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% — the largest single-meeting upward revision in the Bank's history. The RBA has built credibility through consecutive hikes, but is now approaching terminal and will begin signalling cuts for 2027 in the November SMP. The structural credibility path: BoJ ratcheting higher (normalisation credibility builds) while RBA plateaus and eventually pivots dovish (terminal credibility fades). This directional shift in guidance trajectories is pair-bearish over 1-6 months.
key_bilateral_metrics: Guidance trajectory: converging (RBA decelerating, BoJ accelerating)
strategic.what_is_priced: The forward curve embeds gradual differential compression. Our view is that the BoJ's credibility premium is under-appreciated relative to the RBA's peak-rate credibility.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical neutral reflects balanced near-term guidance; strategic bearish reflects the shifting credibility dynamic favouring JPY as the BoJ normalisation path builds momentum while the RBA plateaus.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The Iran-US war de-escalation is the dominant event risk for AUD/JPY specifically. The pair's current premium above fundamental fair value is largely attributable to the Iran-war energy asymmetry — any firm ceasefire with Hormuz reopening would remove this premium, generating 300-800 pips of AUD/JPY downside. Trump declared Operation Epic Fury 'concluded' on 5 May and paused Project Freedom on 6 May, but warned 'if they don't agree, the bombing starts.' The binary path: ceasefire firms → Brent to $85 → AUD/JPY to 108-110; ceasefire fails → Brent to $130+ → AUD/JPY toward 117-120. The asymmetry is bearish because the upside scenario (continued disruption) is largely priced while the downside scenario (reopening) is not. Near-term events: 12 May Federal Budget, 14 May WPI, 15 May April Labour Force, 19 May Japan Q1 GDP — a dense cluster.
key_bilateral_metrics: Pair downside on firm ceasefire: 300-800 pips
Pair upside on ceasefire failure: 200-500 pips
Hormuz status: contested, only 2 commercial transits since 4 May
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price ~60% ceasefire holding. The asymmetry is pair-bearish because the energy premium in AUD/JPY is priced but the unwinding of that premium is not.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategic event risks are more balanced. Iran resolution paths range from lasting peace (40%, pair-bearish) to continued disruption (35%, pair-neutral/positive) to re-escalation (15%, pair-mixed) to broader regional conflict (10%, pair-bearish via risk-off). US-Japan trade tensions (auto tariffs) are a chronic tail risk that is pair-negative through JPY weakness but also AUD-negative via global growth drag. China-related event risk (Politburo, property) affects AUD more than JPY, creating downside skew for the pair.
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic option vol likely underprices the bilateral event-risk distribution.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bearish reflects asymmetric Hormuz unwind risk; strategic neutral reflects the broader distribution of event scenarios.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Australia's AAA/Aaa/AAA sovereign profile is materially stronger than Japan's A+/A1/A. The differential is structural and long-standing — Japan's 250% gross debt/GDP vs Australia's ~33% — but it has not been a pair driver in the tactical window. The 12 May Federal Budget is the immediate event; KPMG previews a 'fourth economy' framing with productivity package and personal tax cut to 15%. Japan's fiscal stance under Takaichi (¥120T initial budget + ¥21.3T supplementary) is expansionary, which is modestly inflationary and JPY-supportive via the BoJ reaction function. For AUD/JPY over 4 weeks, fiscal is a non-factor.
key_bilateral_metrics: Net fiscal signal: neutral for the pair
tactical.what_is_priced: Both fiscal profiles are priced; no mispricing identified.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the fiscal differential creates a modest asymmetric upside for AUD in global fiscal-stress scenarios (flight to quality within G10). If JGB yields break 3% sustainably as the BoJ normalises, the fiscal-debt dynamic could constrain BoJ hawkishness — modestly pair-positive by slowing differential compression. The net strategic fiscal signal is neutral with minor upside skew for the pair in stress scenarios.
key_bilateral_metrics: BoJ constraint risk at JGB 3%: moderate
Net strategic fiscal signal: neutral with minor AUD upside skew in stress

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral with high confidence. Fiscal dynamics are structural background rather than pair drivers.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/JPY's trend regime from price data is bullishly stacked (spot 113.55 > 50d 112.34 > 100d 111.30 > 200d 107.04), supporting trend-following systematic longs. However, the pair is at the 96th percentile of its 52-week range, just 1.0% below the 114.71 cycle high — a classic exhaustion zone. TradingView technical commentary notes the pair is 'locally overbought' after retesting horizontal resistance at 113.76, expecting a local bearish correction. On the upside, resistance sits at the 20d SMA area near 113.65 and at the recent upper Bollinger band peak near 114.75. Support levels: 112.50 (lower Bollinger), 111.97 (TradingView pivot support), 110.00 (psychological + 50d EMA confluence), 109.65 (100d EMA), 107.00 (200d SMA). AUD/JPY estimated IV at ~10% is below the 1Y median, suggesting options are relatively cheap for hedging. Realised vol has been elevated relative to IV (the intervention-driven moves in USD/JPY spill through), creating a positive variance risk premium for spot traders.
tactical.what_is_priced: Vol markets price modest event premium but not a structural reversal. Options appear cheap for downside hedges given the extreme range position and positioning overhang.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The 18-month rally from 86.05 to 114.71 (+33.3%) is a mature trend by any standard. The 200d SMA at 107.04 marks the bull/bear regime line — a test of this level over 1-6 months has approximately 35% probability given the catalyst calendar. If the BoJ delivers two hikes (to 1.25%) and Brent normalises, AUD/JPY could target 105-108 — a test of the 200d SMA from above. The carry-to-vol ratio of 0.36, while still attractive, has compressed from historical 0.50+ levels that typically sustain carry inflows — the 'carry goldilocks' is fading.
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic vol pricing assumes orderly trending continuation. The mature-trend exhaustion pattern at 114+ argues for fatter left tail than currently priced.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical neutral reflects the well-defined bullish MA structure; strategic bearish reflects the mature-trend exhaustion and narrowing carry-to-vol attractiveness.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: AUD/JPY estimated IV at ~10% is below the 1Y median — a below-median vol regime at the 96th percentile of the 52-week price range, with bullish MA stacking but classic exhaustion zone characteristics. Realised vol has been elevated relative to IV, generating a positive variance risk premium.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: Spot expression for directional views is efficient given low IV. For downside hedging, 1M AUD/JPY puts struck at 110-111 offer asymmetric payoff into the June BoJ meeting — if the BoJ hikes and AUD positioning unwinds simultaneously, 300-500 pip moves are possible but not priced. For carry expression, spot long remains the most efficient vehicle given low IV, though the 28-pip breakeven means carry provides negligible cushion against adverse moves.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The June 16-17 BoJ MPM is the dominant event for expression choice. If holding through the event, buying vol via options is rational given cheap IV. Pre-event spot positions should carry tight stops (below 111.00 for longs). Post-June BoJ, the vol surface will likely reprice — any BoJ hike would expand IV from ~10% toward 12-14%, making post-hike options more expensive.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: AUD/JPY is a major G10 cross with good liquidity during Asian and European sessions. Typical bid-offer is 2-4 pips in normal conditions, widening to 8-15 pips around major data events. Cross-currency execution settles through USD; no material liquidity premium vs direct USD pairs.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross; reduce USD factor by -0.02 tactical and -0.02 strategic to reflect lower direct USD funding sensitivity

tactical.bilateral_analysis: As a non-USD cross, AUD/JPY's direct sensitivity to USD funding conditions is lower than for outright USD pairs, but the vehicle-currency channel matters: most AUD/JPY execution settles through USD, and any USD funding stress affects the cross-currency basis on both legs. SOFR is stable at ~3.625%, no money-market stress reported, and the Fed-BoJ swap line remains available but unused. The Warsh confirmation (expected week of 11 May) introduces a regime-change element: a more dovish Fed under Warsh would weaken USD, which historically benefits both AUD and JPY but with different magnitudes. AUD's risk-on character means it gains more from USD weakness during benign conditions (pair-positive), while JPY's safe-haven character means it gains more from USD weakness during stress conditions (pair-negative). Net: neutral for the pair.
key_bilateral_metrics: Warsh confirmation: expected week of 11 May
Dollar smile position: middle (neutral for cross)
tactical.what_is_priced: Benign USD funding priced. No pair-specific mispricing.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategic USD liquidity dynamics are neutral for this cross. A Warsh-led Fed delivering 25-50bp of cuts would weaken USD against both AUD and JPY, with the pair impact depending on relative beta: in risk-on USD weakness, AUD gains more (pair-positive); in recession-driven USD weakness, JPY gains more (pair-negative). The net expected impact is approximately flat. The cross-currency basis for AUD/JPY specifically is not directly observable but can be inferred as the sum of the two USD legs — both are in normal ranges, suggesting no funding stress premium in the pair.
key_bilateral_metrics: Warsh policy bias: more dovish than Powell
Net USD-channel impact on pair: approximately flat
strategic.what_is_priced: Benign funding conditions priced across both legs. No actionable mispricing for this cross.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral. The USD funding channel is secondary for AUD/JPY vs the dominant bilateral drivers (carry, ToT, risk appetite).

adjustments.reason: Pure risk proxy pair; boost risk factor +0.02 tactical and +0.02 strategic to reflect AUD/JPY's archetypal G10 risk barometer status
Commodity-sensitive bilateral ToT; boost tactical weight by +0.02 to reflect active Iran-war energy asymmetry as a real-time pair driver (strategic weight unchanged at 0.16)
Non-USD cross; reduce USD factor by -0.02 tactical and -0.02 strategic to reflect lower direct USD funding sensitivity

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score +0.249 maps to a Neutral stance with a mild bullish tilt. The score is meaningfully positive but below the +0.50 threshold for high conviction, reflecting the tension between the dominant carry/ToT tailwinds (Factors 1, 2, 5 contributing +0.342) and the positioning/event-risk headwinds (Factors 4, 7 contributing -0.093).
tactical.rationale: The tactical horizon for AUD/JPY is dominated by the +360bp carry advantage — the widest in G10 crosses — combined with the Iran-war energy asymmetry that creates a double tailwind for the pair (Australia benefits, Japan suffers). These two factors account for approximately 70% of the positive weighted score. However, the offsetting forces are material: CFTC AUD longs at the 88th percentile represent the most stretched positioning in over a year, while the asymmetric Hormuz unwind risk means the energy premium could evaporate faster than it was built. The standalone dossier views partially conflict: the AUD dossier is tactically neutral/mild bullish while the JPY dossier is tactically bullish JPY (bearish for AUD/JPY) — the net bilateral signal is close to flat, with carry providing the tiebreaker. The path of least resistance is sideways consolidation in the 112-115 range, with the 14 May WPI and 15 May Labour Force releases providing the next directional catalyst. BoJ Summary of Opinions (released ~7 May) could shift the June hike probability and catalyse the first significant pair move. Key catalysts: 12 May Federal Budget, 14 May WPI, 15 May April Labour Force, 19 May Japan Q1 GDP, 23 May Tokyo CPI. Conviction is capped at 3/5 because the distribution is genuinely wide — a BoJ June surprise or Hormuz reopening would trigger 200-500 pip downside not currently priced.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/JPY ranks as the 2nd most attractive carry-long expression among AUD crosses (behind AUD/CHF on carry-to-vol but ahead of AUD/NZD on absolute carry), and approximately 4th among all JPY shorts (behind USD/JPY on liquidity, GBP/JPY on absolute carry, and CAD/JPY on positioning cleanliness). The pair's crowded AUD positioning is its primary disadvantage vs alternatives like CAD/JPY or NZD/JPY.

conditions: Hormuz remains partially closed, Brent stable $95-110
RBA pauses stance confirmed at June meeting
BoJ holds with hawkish language, no escalation of dissent
base_case.path: Consolidation-then-directional: first two weeks range-bound 112-114.50 as market digests Budget/WPI/Labour Force cluster. Mid-month break direction set by the data flow. Pair drifts sideways with modest carry accrual, ending at ~113.00.

conditions: Q1 WPI prints 3.6%, April Labour Force strong
BoJ Summary of Opinions less hawkish than feared
Soft AU data, BoJ signals imminent June hike

tactical.path_expectation: We expect a consolidation-then-directional pattern over the 4-week tactical horizon. The first two weeks are likely range-bound between 112 and 114.50 as the market digests the dense data cluster of Budget (12 May), WPI (14 May), and Labour Force (15 May). Mid-month, the direction will be set by the balance of this data flow: if WPI surprises firm at 3.6% and Hormuz remains disrupted, the pair tests 114.71 and potentially breaks toward 116-117. If WPI disappoints or Hormuz reopens, the pair breaks 112 support toward 110. Volatility is expected to be moderate with 100-150 pip daily ranges around event days, expanding toward 200+ pips on a BoJ catalyst. The carry of approximately 32 pips over 28 days provides minimal directional cushion but is a mild positive for patient holders.

increase_conditions: WPI prints 3.6% AND Brent stable at $105 AND BoJ Summary of Opinions less hawkish than feared → increase to 4
All above PLUS AUD positioning normalises below 70k contracts AND risk-on acceleration → increase to 5
decrease_conditions: Hormuz reopens with Brent sustaining below $90 OR BoJ signals imminent June hike → decrease to 2
BoJ June hike AND Hormuz reopens AND soft AU data simultaneously → decrease to 1

risk_reward.expected_value: (50% × -55) + (25% × +295) + (25% × -355) = -27.5 + 73.75 - 88.75 = -42.5 pips gross; adding carry of ~32 pips reduces net EV to approximately -11 pips — essentially flat
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Mildly unfavourable. The negative expected value reflects the crowded positioning and event-risk skew. Carry of ~32 pips over 4 weeks partially offsets, reducing the net EV to approximately -11 pips — the distribution is roughly symmetric but with fatter left tail from the Hormuz/BoJ binary risks.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score -0.159 maps to Neutral with mild bearish tilt. The strategic horizon flips the tactical picture: the carry advantage neutralises (Factor 1 goes to 0) as the differential compresses, while the bearish factors (positioning unwind, guidance convergence, mature trend, risk-appetite vulnerability) accumulate. No single factor is strongly bearish, but the weight of evidence tilts mildly against the pair.
strategic.rationale: The strategic case for AUD/JPY turns neutral-to-mildly-bearish as the three pillars that support the tactical bull case erode over 1-6 months. First, the RBA-BoJ differential compresses from +360bp toward +310bp as the BoJ delivers 1-2 hikes while the RBA approaches terminal — the carry advantage persists in absolute terms but is no longer expanding. Second, if the Iran ceasefire holds (60% probability), Brent reverts to $80-90 and the bilateral energy asymmetry that has been the pair's most powerful tailwind fades substantially. Third, AUD positioning at the 88th percentile needs to normalise, and the structural narrative supporting long AUD/JPY weakens as the catalyst calendar unfolds. The BoJ credibility channel is a strategic headwind: the April Outlook Report's 2.8% CPI revision is the largest in the Bank's history, signalling a multi-quarter normalisation path that the market has not fully repriced. The path of least resistance is a phased decline: 113-115 consolidation in May-June, directional move lower in Q3 as BoJ hikes and Hormuz normalises, with a target range of 105-112 by Q4. The wide range reflects the genuinely binary distribution — if the Iran ceasefire fails and the BoJ disappoints, the pair could rally toward 117-120. Conviction is 3/5 reflecting this width.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/JPY slides from tactically 2nd among AUD crosses to approximately 4th-5th strategically, as the rate-differential compression undermines the carry thesis. Among JPY shorts, the pair drops to 5th-6th as BoJ normalisation favours pairs with more durable differentials (e.g., NOK/JPY on Norges Bank terminal) or lower positioning risk (e.g., EUR/JPY on cleaner flow dynamics).

conditions: BoJ delivers one hike (June or July) to 1.00%
RBA pauses, Hormuz partially normalises with Brent to $85-95
AUD positioning normalises from 88th percentile toward 5Y mean
base_case.path: Phase 1 (May-June): Consolidation 112-115 with June 16-17 BoJ MPM and RBA as regime-setting events. Phase 2 (July-September): Directional shift lower as BoJ delivers expected hike(s), Hormuz normalisation progresses, carry advantage compresses. Target 108-112 by end-Q3. Phase 3 (October-November): New equilibrium 105-112 depending on BoJ second hike and RBA 2027 cut signals.

conditions: Hormuz remains disrupted, Brent $110+, RBA delivers another hike to 4.60%
BoJ holds on growth concerns, China stimulus surprise
Hormuz fully reopens with Brent below $80, BoJ delivers two hikes to 1.25%
China property deterioration, risk-off episode triggers carry unwind

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path expectation follows three distinct phases over 1-6 months. Phase 1 (May-June): The pair consolidates in the 112-115 range, with the June 16-17 BoJ MPM and 16 June RBA meeting as the key regime-setting events. Volatility remains moderate (~10% IV) but event-premium builds into June. Phase 2 (July-September): A directional shift lower materialises as the BoJ delivers the expected hike(s), Hormuz normalisation progresses toward our 60% base-case probability, and the carry advantage compresses by 25-50bp. Target 108-112 by end-Q3, with pullback levels at 112, 110, and 107 (200d SMA). Phase 3 (October-November): The pair finds a new equilibrium in the 105-112 range depending on whether the BoJ delivers a second hike and whether the RBA begins signalling 2027 cuts. Volatility gradually rises from current ~10% IV toward 12-14% as binary events cluster in Q3.

increase_conditions: BoJ delivers June hike AND Hormuz reopens AND AUD positioning unwinds below 70k contracts → increase to 4 (bearish)
All above PLUS China property deterioration AND risk-off episode materialises → increase to 5 (bearish)
decrease_conditions: Hormuz remains disrupted AND BoJ holds through Q3 AND RBA hikes again → decrease to 2
All above PLUS global risk-on acceleration AND China stimulus → decrease to 1

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Moderately favourable for short AUD/JPY at the strategic horizon despite carrying negative carry. The positive EV is driven by the outsized downside-case payoff (+1,055 pips if BoJ delivers two hikes and Hormuz reopens), which more than offsets the carry cost and upside-case loss.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: ExchangeRates.org.uk weighted bank consensus projects AUD/JPY at ~99.20 early 2026 and 99.65 late 2026; Long Forecast projects AUD/JPY rising to 117.73 by end of May 2026 and reaching 123.04 by December 2026; one forecast source expects AUD/JPY at 100-101 by year-end 2026
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Neutral/mild bullish; 112-114.71 base case over 4 weeks, ending ~113.00
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Neutral/mild bearish; 108-112 base case by November 2026
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: Above the most bearish bank forecasts (sub-100) but below the most bullish algorithmic forecasts (120+); in the middle-to-lower range of published forecasts
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Approximately +900-1,300 pips above the most bearish bank consensus (sub-100) and approximately 800-1,100 pips below the most bullish algorithmic forecasts (123+)
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We differ from the most bullish algorithmic forecasts (120+) because they extrapolate the current energy premium without adequately pricing the BoJ normalisation path and the Hormuz resolution probability. We differ from the most bearish bank forecasts (sub-100) because the +310bp residual carry provides meaningful floor support even after compression. The key analytical difference is that we weigh the BoJ's 6-3 hawkish dissent more heavily than consensus, viewing it as a credible signal of faster normalisation that will compress the differential by 50-75bp by year-end. We also treat the Hormuz binary more symmetrically than both bull and bear consensus — the 60% reopening probability is a material input that algorithmic models do not capture and that bank forecasts may not have updated for the Iran-war dynamic.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Strategic short at 113-114 targeting 108-110 generates +300-600 pips net after carry costs
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If algorithmic bull case materialises (120+), mark-to-market loss of 600-700 pips on short position

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic stances are mildly divergent, reflecting the inflection point in the pair's structural drivers. The carry advantage is at peak attractiveness right now but compresses over the strategic horizon. The energy premium persists tactically but erodes strategically with 60% probability of Hormuz normalisation over 6 months. The BoJ normalisation path, AUD positioning mean-reversion, and oil normalisation are all directionally bearish for the pair but operate on a 3-6 month lag — hence the tactical-strategic split.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: June 16-17 BoJ MPM: a hike converts both horizons to bearish; a dovish hold extends the tactical bullish case into July
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Tactical carry longs can be held into mid-June with tight stops below 111.00; strategic shorts should be layered on at 114+ with stops above 115.50 and targets of 108-110 by Q3.

forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: +160 pips in favour of short (forward prices 112.60, we expect 111.00)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +170 pips in favour of short (forward prices 111.70, we expect 110.00)

catalysts.rationale: Tight fiscal would support AUD modestly via reduced bond supply pressure and fiscal consolidation narrative reinforcing RBA credibility; expansionary stance would marginally weaken AUD through increased issuance and fiscal risk. Budget impact on AUD/JPY typically 30-80 pips. The Federal Budget sets the fiscal context for the RBA's June decision and frames the growth outlook that influences the RBA's terminal rate path.

WPI is the RBA's most-watched wage measure and the primary determinant of whether the Bank can pause or must continue hiking. A print above 3.6% YoY extends the hawkish RBA path and widens the carry differential, supporting AUD/JPY by 80-120 pips. Consensus is 3.3-3.5%. An upside surprise signals wage-inflation entrenchment, forcing the RBA to maintain its hawkish stance well into 2026 and reinforcing the rate-differential advantage against the BoJ's gradual normalisation.

Strong employment (+15k, unemployment at or below 4.3%) supports RBA hawkishness and extends the rate differential advantage for AUD/JPY. A weak print (unemployment rising to 4.5% or above) would trigger positioning unwind and be pair-negative by opening the door to an RBA pause and potentially faster rate cuts. The bilateral transmission is through the RBA reaction function — Australian unemployment data directly determines the speed of differential compression.

Weak Q1 GDP growth could signal BoJ caution and delay hike timing, which would be pair-positive by preserving the carry differential. Strong growth supports the BoJ normalisation path (pair-negative by accelerating differential compression). The BoJ cut its FY2026 GDP forecast to 0.5%, setting a low bar. The bilateral transmission is direct: Japanese growth data determines the BoJ's confidence in the normalisation path, which is the primary driver of rate-differential compression for AUD/JPY.

Tokyo CPI is the leading indicator for national CPI and the most important monthly inflation signal for the BoJ's June decision. A reading above 2.0% (consensus ~1.5-1.7%) would accelerate BoJ June hike probability, compressing the rate differential and generating pair-negative pressure of 80-150 pips. A reading below 1.5% would reduce June hike probability (pair-positive). The bilateral importance is heightened because the BoJ's June hike decision rests substantially on the inflation trajectory, and Tokyo CPI is the last major data point before the June 16-17 MPM.

Westpac nowcasts Q4 at +1.1% QoQ — the strongest non-pandemic quarterly print since 2016. A strong print reinforces RBA hawkish stance and delays the rate-cut timeline, which is pair-positive by maintaining the carry differential. A weak print accelerates RBA dovish pivot expectations and compresses the differential faster. The bilateral transmission is through the RBA reaction function — Australian GDP growth is the key input for whether the RBA can sustain the carry advantage against an accelerating BoJ normalisation path.

Bullock's language signals June pause with approximately 80% probability. A pause marks the peak of the RBA hawkish cycle, reducing carry momentum and signalling the beginning of differential compression — pair-negative at the margin as it confirms the RBA is no longer widening the advantage. A surprise hike to 4.60% would be sharply pair-positive (+100-150 pips). The bilateral relevance is that the RBA and BoJ meet on the same week (16-17 June), creating a bilateral event cluster that determines the short-term trajectory of the rate differential.

Three April dissenters (Nakagawa, Takata, Tamura) at 1.00% make a June hike live at approximately 35-40% probability. A hike from 0.75% to 1.00% would shock AUD/JPY 150-250 pips lower by compressing the rate differential by 25bp, triggering AUD long liquidation from crowded positions, and validating the BoJ normalisation narrative. A dovish hold would send the pair testing 115+. This is the single most important catalyst for AUD/JPY over the entire strategic horizon — it simultaneously determines the carry differential, the credibility narrative, and the positioning unwind catalyst.

catalysts.event: BoJ MPM with Quarterly Outlook Report
catalysts.rationale: If June was a hold, July becomes the dominant hike candidate as the BoJ's full Outlook Report provides the framework for further normalisation. The quarterly FY2026/27 CPI revision will signal the multi-quarter normalisation path. A July hike (second hike to 1.00% or first from 0.75%) would continue differential compression. The bilateral transmission for AUD/JPY is direct and powerful — each 25bp of BoJ hiking historically generates 300-400 pips of AUD/JPY downside when accompanied by a pausing RBA.

The RBA's most-watched data release. A trimmed mean at 3.7% or above forces further hike consideration, is pair-positive by maintaining carry differential. Below 3.3% confirms disinflation and opens the door to RBA rate-cut signalling — pair-negative by accelerating differential compression from the AUD side simultaneously with BoJ normalisation from the JPY side. The bilateral significance is that Q2 CPI arriving in late July alongside potential BoJ July meeting creates a dual-catalyst event for the rate differential.

invalidators.condition: AUD/JPY breaks above 117.00 on a weekly close
invalidators.mechanism: A decisive break of 117.00 on a weekly close would signal that the rate-gap expansion overrides positioning concerns, trend-following CTA capital adds to longs, the 2007-era cycle highs (~107-110) have been decisively cleared, and the energy premium has been reinforced rather than eroded. The technical regime changes from consolidation to acceleration, and the crowded positioning concern dissipates as momentum attracts new buyers.

invalidators.condition: BoJ June MPM delivers dovish hold (8-1 vote, no dissent escalation) AND removes 'real rates significantly low' language
invalidators.mechanism: A dovish BoJ hold invalidates the BoJ normalisation thesis that underpins the strategic bearish case. The carry differential persists at +360bp, positioning normalises on the JPY side removing the safe-haven headwind, and the June hike risk premium in options unwinds — all pair-positive. The BoJ stepping back from the hawkish threshold signals a longer runway for the carry trade.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BoJ statement vote split and key language changes

invalidators.condition: Brent crude returns below $80 AND AUD/JPY holds above 112 for 5+ trading days
invalidators.mechanism: If AUD/JPY demonstrates resilience above 112 even after the energy premium is removed (Brent <$80), it signals the carry floor is structural and self-sustaining without the commodity tailwind. This would upgrade confidence in the neutral base case and reduce the probability weight on the bearish scenario, as the pair has found fundamental support beyond the energy asymmetry.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Brent crude daily close + AUD/JPY spot

invalidators.condition: AUD/JPY breaks below 107.00 (200d SMA) on a weekly close
invalidators.mechanism: A decisive weekly close below the 200d SMA at 107.04 changes the trend regime from bullish to bearish. CTA trend-following flows reverse from long to short, the bull/bear regime line breaks, carry-trade unwind accelerates, and the structural narrative of the pair shifts from 'well-supported carry trade' to 'broken carry unwind.' The 200d SMA is the last structural support for the entire 18-month bull trend.

invalidators.condition: Iron ore sustains below $90/t AND Brent below $85 simultaneously for 5+ trading days
invalidators.mechanism: Simultaneous deterioration in both major commodity drivers for Australia removes the bilateral terms-of-trade advantage entirely. Iron ore below $90 signals China demand weakness (pair-negative via AUD ToT) while Brent below $85 removes the Iran-war energy premium (pair-negative via Japan import-cost normalisation). The bilateral commodity edge that has been the non-carry driver of AUD/JPY outperformance evaporates, and only a 310bp carry residual (after BoJ hikes) would remain to support the pair.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Iron ore 62% Fe CFR China price + Brent crude daily close

signposts.confirms_if: Holds above +230bp — carry intact and differential not compressing faster than expected
signposts.invalidates_if: Compresses below +200bp — BoJ aggressive normalisation or RBA early cut signals accelerating differential compression beyond base case
signposts.bilateral_importance: The AU-JP 10Y spread is the direct driver of carry-trade flows into AUD/JPY. A narrowing spread mechanically reduces the income advantage of holding AUD vs JPY, triggering position unwind from the yield-sensitive institutional investor base. This is the single most important signpost for monitoring the pair's primary structural support mechanism.

signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above $100/bbl — energy asymmetry intact, bilateral ToT advantage preserved
signposts.invalidates_if: Sustains below $85/bbl for 10+ trading days — energy premium removed, bilateral commodity edge erodes
signposts.bilateral_importance: Brent crude is the most powerful pair-specific driver for AUD/JPY given Australia's exporter status and Japan's 95.9% Middle East crude dependency. No other G10 pair has this degree of asymmetric energy transmission. A $15 move in Brent generates an estimated 200-400 pip AUD/JPY response through the bilateral import-cost and export-revenue channels simultaneously.

signposts.confirms_if: Normalises toward +50-60k contracts — overcrowding resolved, technical overhang reduced
signposts.invalidates_if: Builds above +110k contracts — extreme crowding reaches historically unprecedented levels, increasing crash risk
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD-side positioning is the primary driver of AUD/JPY's unwind risk profile. At the 88th percentile, the crowded long creates asymmetric downside: any catalyst triggers AUD liquidation rather than gradual adjustment. This signpost monitors the pair's most significant non-fundamental risk — mechanical position flow that could generate 300-500 pip moves independent of fundamental developments.

signposts.current_level: ~10bp of hike priced for June (35-40% hike probability)
signposts.confirms_if: June hike probability rises above 50% — differential compression accelerating, pair-bearish confirmation
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 20% — BoJ caution dominant, normalisation timeline extends, carry advantage preserved longer
signposts.bilateral_importance: BoJ rate path is the primary driver of differential compression for AUD/JPY. The BoJ is the only central bank that can materially change the pair's rate landscape over the tactical-to-strategic horizon — the RBA is near terminal while the BoJ is still normalising. OIS-implied hike probability is the real-time market pricing of this compression risk.

signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above $100/t — Australian ToT support intact, AUD income channel active
signposts.invalidates_if: Breaks below $90/t for 5+ trading days — China demand weakness, AUD terms-of-trade deteriorate
signposts.bilateral_importance: Iron ore is the primary direct AUD terms-of-trade driver and the second most important commodity signpost after Brent for this pair. Japan is a secondary iron ore consumer, so the transmission is predominantly through the AUD leg rather than bilateral. At $105-108, iron ore is providing approximately 100-200 pips of support to AUD/JPY above pure rate-differential fair value.

signposts.confirms_if: Below 16 — risk-on conditions support carry; AUD/JPY maintains high-beta positive correlation to equities
signposts.invalidates_if: Above 25 — risk-off triggers carry unwind; AUD/JPY historically declines 3-5% per 10-point VIX spike
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/JPY is the purest G10 risk barometer and exhibits the highest carry-trade sensitivity to VIX in the G10 cross universe. A VIX spike above 25 triggers simultaneous AUD selling (risk-off exit) and JPY buying (safe-haven demand) — creating a double-whammy pair decline that amplifies beyond what either leg alone would suggest. The August 2024 carry unwind (AUD/JPY -15 figures) was driven by VIX spiking above 65.

assumptions: AUD/JPY pair-specific implied volatility, risk reversals, and cross-currency basis swap data are not directly available from free public sources. IV is estimated from the component AUD/USD and USD/JPY legs with correlation adjustment. Precise OTC quotes should be sourced from Bloomberg/Refinitiv terminals for trade execution.
Forward points are estimated from the bilateral rate differential of +360bp; precise AUD/JPY forward points should be verified with dealer quotes as cross-currency forwards include basis adjustments.

Weight adjustments reflect AUD/JPY's pure risk-proxy character (Factor 3 boosted by +0.02 tactical/strategic) and non-USD cross status (Factor 10 reduced by -0.02 tactical/strategic), with Factor 5 boosted by +0.02 tactical for the active commodity-ToT driver. The sum remains exactly 1.00 for both horizons.
Bilateral trade data uses 2025 annual figures from COMTRADE; 2026 partial-year data reflects the Iran-war disruption and may not be representative of normalised flows.

PPP fair value of AUD/JPY at ~98 is derived from the AUD dossier's OECD-based estimate; the deviation of +15.9% represents the cumulative overvaluation from carry, commodity, and risk-appetite channels.

The BoJ's June hike probability of 35-40% is our assessment based on the three-way hawkish dissent and Ueda's 'real rates significantly low' language; market pricing (OIS-implied) is somewhat lower at approximately 10bp implied.

The Hormuz reopening probability of 60% over 6 months is a qualitative assessment based on the geopolitical signals (Trump's Operation Epic Fury 'concluded' declaration and Project Freedom pause on 5-6 May); this binary determines a significant portion of the pair's asymmetric risk distribution.

AUD/JPY carry-to-vol ratio of 0.36 is calculated as annualised carry of 360bp divided by estimated 3M IV of ~10%; this estimate should be updated with live vol surface data for precise risk management.
